title: Free-threading support: StopTheWorld synchronization + per-object locking
author: devdanzin
body:
## Context

guppy3's heapy module walks the interpreter's heap, thread states, and frame stacks — operations that read data owned by other threads. Under free-threaded Python (PEP 703), these operations need explicit synchronization. The sets module needs per-object critical sections.

This issue tracks the full set of changes needed to make guppy3 safe under free-threaded Python. A detailed synchronization guide is available at https://gist.github.com/devdanzin/c430e676ac2c27b30af644847af9aa1b (produced by [ft-review-toolkit](https://github.com/devdanzin/ft-review-toolkit) agents).

### Key insight: the STW contract on 3.14+

On Python 3.14+ free-threading builds, **object allocation does NOT trigger GC** — GC runs only on the eval breaker in the bytecode loop, which doesn't execute during StopTheWorld. This means `PyList_New`, `PyDict_New`, `PyUnicode_FromString`, `Py_BuildValue`, etc. are all safe during STW. The only hard constraint is: **no Python code invocation** (`__hash__`, `__eq__`, `__getattr__`, `__str__`, `__del__`). Dict operations are safe if keys/values are `CheckExact`-verified builtins.

This significantly simplifies the implementation — worklists can use `PyList`, results can be built during STW, and the two-phase "collect C data during STW / build Python objects after" pattern is only needed for operations that genuinely invoke Python code.

## Tasks

### 1. `rootstate.c` — Interpreter/thread state walking [CRITICAL]

**Functions**: `rootstate_relate_unlocked`, `rootstate_traverse_unlocked`, `rootstate_getattr_unlocked`, `rootstate_dir_unlocked` (lines 314–895)

These walk the interpreter and thread state linked lists (interps, threads, frames, dicts). Currently protected only by `HEAD_LOCK`, which guards list membership but not the contents being read. Without the GIL, another thread could be pushing/popping frames, modifying `ts->current_exception`, or changing `ts->dict` concurrently.

**Fix**: Wrap each function in `_PyEval_StopTheWorld(interp)` / `_PyEval_StartTheWorld(interp)`. On 3.14+, the allocation calls these functions make (`PyUnicode_FromFormat`, `PyList_Append`, `Py_VISIT`) are all safe during STW, so no two-phase split is needed — the entire function body can run with the world stopped.

### 2. `hv.c`, `hv_cli_dictof.c` — Full heap traversal [CRITICAL]

**Functions**: `hv_heap` / `NyHeapView_iterate` (hv.c:857–964), `hv_cli_dictof_update` (hv_cli_dictof.c:80–138)

Heap traversal follows references recursively using `tp_traverse`. Without STW, objects can be deallocated or mutated during traversal. `hv_cli_dictof_update` calls `gc.get_objects()`, then iterates the result.

**Fix**: `_PyEval_StopTheWorld` for the traversal phase. Since allocation is safe during STW on 3.14+, the `PyList`-based worklist can remain as-is (no C-level stack replacement needed). `NyNodeGraph_AddEdge` and `PyList_Append` are safe. `gc.get_objects()` invokes Python so it must be called *before* STW.

For dict-based memoization during STW, use `CheckExact` guards on keys/values to avoid `__hash__`/`__eq__` callbacks — this approach was confirmed correct.

### 3. `horizon.c` — Linked list + PyRefTracer race [HIGH]

**Lines**: 33–85, 171–260

The Horizon linked list is already well-protected with `PyMutex rm_mutex`. Two remaining issues:

- **Install/uninstall race** (lines 62–85): The `PyRefTracer_GetTracer` check is racy (noted in a code comment). Fix: keep `rm_mutex` held during `horizon_install`/`horizon_uninstall`.
- **`rm.replaced` flag** (line 55): Written from the tracer callback, read in `horizon_news`. Fix: use `_Py_atomic_store_int` / `_Py_atomic_load_int`.

### 4. `bitset.c` — `_Py_ForgetReference` realloc optimization [HIGH]

**Lines**: 892–978

The realloc-in-place optimization uses `_Py_ForgetReference` and `_Py_DecRefTotal` — internal CPython APIs whose behavior under free-threading is not guaranteed. The `PyUnstable_Object_IsUniquelyReferenced` guard is necessary but may not be sufficient.

**Fix**: Either restructure to avoid internal APIs (allocate new → copy → decref old), or wrap in `Py_BEGIN_CRITICAL_SECTION(self)` and verify behavior on each CPython release.

### 5. `stdtypes.c` — Frame/type traversal [HIGH, inherits STW]

**Lines**: 380–460, 586–666

Frame traversal reads `_PyInterpreterFrame` internals; type traversal reads `tp_dict`, `tp_subclasses`, `tp_mro`. These are called as part of heap traversal (task 2), so they inherit STW protection from the caller. No additional synchronization needed.

**Note**: `NyStaticType_GetState` accesses `type->tp_subclasses` as a raw integer index — this CPython internal detail may differ under free-threading and needs testing.

### 6. `hv_cli_prod.c` — `sizeof_PyGC_Head` lazy init [MEDIUM]

**Lines**: 25–78

Classic TOCTOU on a static variable. The initialization calls `PyImport_ImportModule`, which can release the GIL.

**Fix**: Double-checked locking with `PyMutex` + `_Py_atomic_load_ssize_relaxed` / `_Py_atomic_store_ssize_relaxed`.

### 7. `impsets.c` — `nodeset_exports` import [LOW, already correct]

Already uses `PyMutex` with double-checked locking. Minor improvement: add `_Py_atomic_load_ptr_relaxed` / `_Py_atomic_store_ptr_relaxed` for formal correctness on ARM.

### 8. Sets module — Per-object critical sections

Add `Py_BEGIN_CRITICAL_SECTION(self)` / `Py_END_CRITICAL_SECTION(self)` to all methods that mutate set state. This is the easiest module to make thread-safe.

### 9. GIL declarations — Phased migration

Both modules currently declare `Py_MOD_GIL_USED` (correct for now). Recommended phasing:
1. Sets module: add critical sections → switch to `Py_MOD_GIL_NOT_USED`
2. Heapyc: add STW to traversal/rootstate → switch to `Py_MOD_GIL_NOT_USED` when complete

## Debug tooling suggestion

An `assert_world_stopped()` / `assert_world_running()` annotation system (similar to Linux kernel's `might_sleep()`) would catch transitive STW violations at debug time. A possible implementation using `_Thread_local` is described in the synchronization guide linked above.

## Recommended implementation order

1. **Sets module** (easiest, self-contained) — quick win, builds confidence
2. **Easy heapy fixes** — `sizeof_PyGC_Head` mutex, `horizon.c` atomics, `impsets.c` atomic reads
3. **Debug annotations** — `assert_world_stopped()` / `assert_world_running()`
4. **rootstate.c** — hardest but most impactful, wrap in STW
5. **Heap traversal** — wrap in STW, `gc.get_objects()` before STW
6. **Switch heapyc to `Py_MOD_GIL_NOT_USED`** — only after all above is in place

---

<sub>This issue was drafted and created by [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code) and reviewed by a human.</sub>

comments.author: zhuyifei1999
comments.body:
Oops I just saw the issue creation, sorry.

> This significantly simplifies the implementation — worklists can use PyList

I'm still thinking about this. I think there's still a benefit to using worklists, where, since it only stays for the lifetime of the stop-the-world I won't need to refcount. Considering refcounting in freethreading is lots of atomics and somewhat complex logic, this could be a lot of performance savings... 

... but I'd have to code it correctly... I don't think a dynamically-expanding array is that difficult if at all (and pretty sure it's not).

> hv_cli_dictof_update calls gc.get_objects(), then iterates the result.

I don't know / remember why hv_cli_dictof_update does this. I need to re-read the code. Ideally I could rewrite it so that it doesn't invoke the GC. (No other classifier does this, I don't why this one is an exception)

> The PyRefTracer_GetTracer check is racy (noted in a code comment). 

The racy comment was actually referring to other threads that may call PyRefTracer_GetTracer through other means, for example, tracemalloc. I think making horizon not race with itself makes sense but I did not think about it at the time.

> Fix: use _Py_atomic_store_int / _Py_atomic_load_int.
> Minor improvement: add _Py_atomic_load_ptr_relaxed / _Py_atomic_store_ptr_relaxed for formal correctness on ARM.

These are nice-to-haves. For the `rm.replaced` the worst case that can happen is getting data that is stale. The atomic API are all CPython private APIs, annoyingly (looks like there has been requests to add to public API: https://discuss.python.org/t/exposing-atomic-operations-tooling-in-the-public-c-api/39973/9 https://discuss.python.org/t/free-threading-expose-atomic-operations/101660/6). I might try to use C11 atomics and hope it works on the builders.

I was reading the report the other day and I saw some parts where I feel like might miss some details:

> Py_INCREF / Py_DECREF / Py_XINCREF / Py_XDECREF — atomic refcount ops

The DECREF ones are safe only if it's not the final reference. because if it is, it could call __del__. Even if it's a built-in type, like say, list, it could still contain user stuffs and end up calling __del__ on them.

Though, I don't think heapyc should have a code path to do that. It's trying to observe world state, not mutating it by deleting existing references. 

> PyUnicode_FromFormat — string creation

As long as no %R or %S that could enter custom types I think? Are unicodes safe when repr-ed? I'm not sure if it enters locale logic, in which case would be unsafe

comments.author: zhuyifei1999
comments.body:
> 6. hv_cli_prod.c — sizeof_PyGC_Head lazy init [MEDIUM]

Actually I just remembered. In free-threading this should just return 0. It's not used there https://github.com/python/cpython/commit/587d4802034749e2aace9c00b00bd73eccdae1e7

comments.author: zhuyifei1999
comments.body:
Looks like guppy3 will be incompatible if `Py_DEBUG && Py_GIL_DISABLED`, for 3.14.3.

Trying to debug a refcount issue with Py_DEBUG, then I hit this backtrace:

```
#0  0x00007ffff7d5081c in ?? () from /usr/lib64/libc.so.6
#1  0x00007ffff7cf6926 in raise () from /usr/lib64/libc.so.6
#2  0x00007ffff7cde36a in abort () from /usr/lib64/libc.so.6

#4  0x00007ffff7ceedef in __assert_fail () from /usr/lib64/libc.so.6
#5  0x00005555558793d9 in _PyCriticalSection_AssertHeld (mutex=0x7fffb4b9f55a) at cpython/Include/internal/pycore_critical_section.h:226
#6  _Py_dict_lookup (mp=0x7fffb4b9f550, key=0x7fffb653b530, hash=0xa207ef21fd526664, value_addr=0x7ffffffee658) at cpython/Objects/dictobject.c:1254
#7  0x000055555587c1f9 in _PyDict_GetItemRef_KnownHash_LockHeld (op=0x7fffb4b9f550, key=0x7fffb653b530, hash=0xa207ef21fd526664, result=0x7ffffffee728) at cpython/Objects/dictobject.c:2420
#8  0x0000555555d1dc79 in bounded_lru_cache_get_lock_held (self=0x7fffb4ddc950, args=0x7fffb653b530, kwds=0x0, result=<optimized out>, key=<optimized out>, hash=<optimized out>) at cpython/Modules/_functoolsmodule.c:1325
#9  bounded_lru_cache_wrapper (self=0x7fffb4ddc950, args=0x7fffb653b530, kwds=0x0) at cpython/Modules/_functoolsmodule.c:1499
#10 0x0000555555d1c4d6 in lru_cache_call (op=0x7fffb4ddc950, args=0x7fffb653b530, kwds=0x0) at cpython/Modules/_functoolsmodule.c:1652
#11 0x00005555557d6542 in _PyObject_Call (tstate=0x55555604aa80 <_PyRuntime+362816>, callable=0x7fffb4ddc950, args=0x7fffb653b530, kwargs=0x0) at cpython/Objects/call.c:361
#12 0x00005555557d66cc in PyObject_Call (callable=0x7fffb4ddc950, args=0x7fffb653b530, kwargs=0x0) at cpython/Objects/call.c:373
#13 0x0000555555a7e9bc in _PyEval_EvalFrameDefault (tstate=<optimized out>, frame=<optimized out>, throwflag=<optimized out>) at cpython/Python/generated_cases.c.h:2656
```

Basically, _functools.lru_cache believes it's the sole referrer of its dict and proceeds to... not lock the dict.

And since it's pretty trivial to get a dict to participate in GC, and I can `gc.get_objects`... here's a trivial reproducer for Python 3.14.3 (I stole some code from https://docs.python.org/3/library/functools.html#functools.lru_cache):

```
zhuyifei1999@zhuyifei1999-Framework-16 ~/cpython $ cat /tmp/test.py 
from functools import lru_cache
import gc

@lru_cache(maxsize=32)
def get_pep(num):
    return type('', (), {})()

for n in 8, 290, 308, 320, 8, 218, 320, 279, 289, 320, 9991:
    pep = get_pep(n)
    print(n, pep)

objs = gc.get_objects()

for n in 8, 290, 308, 320, 8, 218, 320, 279, 289, 320, 9991:
    pep = get_pep(n)
    print(n, pep)
zhuyifei1999@zhuyifei1999-Framework-16 ~/cpython $ gdb -ex r --args cpython-tsan/bin/python3.14td /tmp/test.py
[...]
Using host libthread_db library "/usr/lib64/libthread_db.so.1".

290 <__main__. object at 0x7fffb4552ea0>

python3.14td: ./Include/internal/pycore_critical_section.h:226: void _PyCriticalSection_AssertHeld(PyMutex *): Assertion `cs != NULL && cs->_cs_mutex == mutex' failed.
```

@devdanzin Is this worth reporting?

That said, I don't think this is a dealbreaker for guppy since we will stop-the-world for all traversal, so while we look at objects, no one else will.

comments.author: zhuyifei1999
comments.body:
Another common code path that is almost always unsafe for STW:

- `PyErr_CheckSignals` may call `_Py_RunGC` (if pending GC flag was set on thread)
- `PyErr_SetFromErrno` may call `PyErr_CheckSignals` (if EINTR)

I don't think it's super relevant to guppy, but it might help spot issues in other cexts in case they use stop-the-world. It means almost anything that interfaces with the OS (which may call PyErr_SetFromErrno) / long running procedures (which may call PyErr_CheckSignals) are illegal for STW...

Though for guppy it's kinda a bummer because I can't interrupt a large heap traversal, unless I find a different way to do it:
https://github.com/zhuyifei1999/guppy3/blob/859957de8ad860ba973917155bbce4fe6fc47a4a/src/heapy/hv.c#L313-L318

comments.author: zhuyifei1999
comments.body:
Performance is struggling. `-DNDEBUG` builds in both cases:

```python
from guppy import hpy, heapy
hpy().test(False)
heapy.heapyc.xmemstats()
```

Non-freethreading: 13.0s, Freethreading: 17.6s

Long traversal test:
```python
class node(object):
    def __init__(self, value, _next=None):
        self.value = value
        self.next = _next

llist = node(0)
curnode = llist
for i in range(int(1e6)):
    curnode.next = node(0)
    curnode = curnode.next

hp = hpy()
print(hp.heap())
print(hp.iso(curnode).rp)
```

Non-freethreading: 3.4s, Freethreading: 15.6s

It's hard to tell how much time was spent on refcounting (since it's split time and inlined a lot, so won't show up on flame graphs), but I think there's merit to this:

> I think there's still a benefit to using worklists, where, since it only stays for the lifetime of the stop-the-world I won't need to refcount.

Especially considering how I can't check for signals anymore.

comments.author: devdanzin
comments.body:
> from functools import lru_cache
> import gc
> 
> @lru_cache(maxsize=32)
> def get_pep(num):
>     return type('', (), {})()
> 
> for n in 8, 290, 308, 320, 8, 218, 320, 279, 289, 320, 9991:
>     pep = get_pep(n)
>     print(n, pep)
> 
> objs = gc.get_objects()
> 
> for n in 8, 290, 308, 320, 8, 218, 320, 279, 289, 320, 9991:
>     pep = get_pep(n)
>     print(n, pep)

> Is this worth reporting?

I was told anything involving `gc.get_objects()` is likely to result in FT issues (https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/132707#issuecomment-2816337717), but if we can find another way of getting that dictionary to participate in GC it's sure an important bug.

I've given Claude the mission of finding another way, will report back when it's finished.

comments.author: zhuyifei1999
comments.body:
> we can find another way of getting that dictionary to participate in GC it's sure an important bug.

I mean, you could just call a lru_cache-ed function with GC-able arguments and that dict will also participate in GC. I think the main issue is more of obtaining a reference to that dict without `gc.get_objects()` (or guppy I guess), and for _functools.lru_cache, I don't think it'd willingly give its cache out anywhere.

> I was told anything involving gc.get_objects() is likely to result in FT issues (https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/132707#issuecomment-2816337717)

I got it repro-ed in rr. This is a tuple being printed, which is obvious from your backtrace, but with rr I can see why it is a bad value.

This is the backtrace of when the tuple was being printed:
```
[#0] 0x7fc55c7a406b → PyUnicodeWriter_WriteRepr(writer=0x313820907c0, obj=0x0)
[#1] 0x7fc55c751ded → tuple_repr(self=(<str_ascii_iterator at remote 0x31362a705d0>, 0x0))
```

(I trimmed the paths a bit because it was way too spammy)

It's trying to print the traceback and for some reason itertools wants to set an item to NULL on an tuple that is participating in GC: https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/v3.14.3/Modules/itertoolsmodule.c#L3867

List I think normally the NULL elements in a new tuple does not leave the function it's constructed in, and since gc.get_objects does a STW which synchronizes all threads, it will never see a tuple half-constructed.

Maybe there's a reason itertools does this. I can't tell (it's not obvious to me what the code is doing without reading more closely).

comments.author: devdanzin
comments.body:
Here's what Claude concluded when trying to avoid `gc`:


> **The question is: what commit is YiFei's build from?** If it's before commit `9ad0c7b0f14` (the gh-132641 fix from around March 2025), this is already fixed. If it's after, something else is going on.
> 
> Could you ask YiFei:
> 1. What's `python3.14td --version` or `git log --oneline -1` on their CPython build?
> 2. Does `git log --oneline Modules/_functoolsmodule.c | head -5` show the gh-132641 fix?
> 
> If the build predates the fix, there's no point trying to find a gc-free reproducer — the bug is already known and the fix is to use the non-`_LockHeld` dict APIs. If it's after the fix, then `gc.get_objects()` is triggering a regression or a different code path, and that would be worth reporting regardless of whether we can remove the gc dependency.
>

comments.author: devdanzin
comments.body:
Here's a reproducer for the `itertools` issue, we should file this as a bug in the same class as https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/146056: 

```python
# Crash in itertools.zip_longest: ittuple contains NULL after iterator exhaustion
# gc.get_referents() exposes the internal tuple, repr() crashes on NULL item
#
# On builds with the PyUnicodeWriter_WriteRepr NULL fix (3.13.x, 3.14.x+),
# this displays <NULL> instead of crashing. On builds without it, segfault.
# Either way, zip_longest should not expose NULLs in a GC-tracked tuple.

import itertools
import gc

zl = itertools.zip_longest(iter([1]), iter([1, 2, 3]))
next(zl)  # (1, 1) — both iterators alive
next(zl)  # (None, 2) — first iterator exhausted, ittuple[0] set to NULL

# The internal ittuple now contains NULL at position 0
# gc.get_referents exposes it
refs = gc.get_referents(zl)
for r in refs:
    if isinstance(r, tuple):
        print(repr(r))  # Segfault or <NULL> depending on build
```

comments.author: zhuyifei1999
comments.body:
```
zhuyifei1999@zhuyifei1999-Framework-16 ~/cpython $ git log --oneline Modules/_functoolsmodule.c | grep 132641
66d6860439c [3.14] gh-132641: fix race in `lru_cache` under free-threading (GH-133787) (#133979)
```

Yes

Looking at that commit, it's more like it's related to the cause of the crash rather than fixing it. The change (https://github.com/python/cpython/commit/66d6860439c#diff-d1deea0fb29260d389bac1b24c3ec3631e843d177e85c0e93d5cf50966dee630R1456) modifies the code to call the unlocked methods, while the unlocked methods assert that the lock has been held, causing the assert fail. Normally this would be find because lru_cache has exclusive ownership over the dict, but with a call to gc, that bet is off.

> Here's a reproducer for the itertools issue, we should file this as a bug in the same class as https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/146056:

Yep, this repro is much nicer. Do you want to file it? I'm not very familiar with filing CPython issues 😅

comments.author: devdanzin
comments.body:
> Do you want to file it? I'm not very familiar with filing CPython issues 😅

Sure, probably tonight.

Here's Claude's analysis, your first reproducer is the `lru_cache` bug that doesn't go away when we fix the `zip_longest`  one:

----
This is a legitimate CPython bug in the lru_cache free-threading fix. The reproducer needs `gc.get_objects()` because only the GC's stop-the-world mechanism creates the state where the assertion detects the lock mismatch (normal single-threaded execution without GC interaction happens to work because there's no contention, but the lock is genuinely not held).

**Summary of what we have — two independent bugs:**

1. **`zip_longest` NULL-in-ittuple** — stores NULL in a GC-tracked tuple when iterators exhaust. Our patch fixes it. Worth filing standalone.

2. **`lru_cache` critical section doesn't lock the cache dict** — `bounded_lru_cache_wrapper` does `Py_BEGIN_CRITICAL_SECTION(self)` but `_PyDict_GetItemRef_KnownHash_LockHeld` asserts `self->cache` is locked. The gh-132641 fix converted the `SetItem` calls to `_LockHeld` variants (assuming `self`'s CS covers the dict), but the assertion checks the dict's own mutex. Fix: change line 1498 to `Py_BEGIN_CRITICAL_SECTION2(self, self->cache)` (and similarly at line 1511). This is a real bug in the gh-132641 fix, reproducible only on debug free-threaded builds because release builds don't have the assertion.

comments.author: zhuyifei1999
comments.body:
> change line 1498 to Py_BEGIN_CRITICAL_SECTION2(self, self->cache) (and similarly at line 1511)

oh, I didn't think about that. Makes sense yeah.

> (normal single-threaded execution without GC interaction happens to work because there's no contention, but the lock is genuinely not held)

Uh, I don't think this is the case. The assertion is bypassed if the object has a refcount of 1:

https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/a95ee3a21d97afdbe6bd2ce4cd8343a36cd13b02/Include/internal/pycore_critical_section.h#L64-L67

`lru_cache` assumes it's the exclusive owner of that dict, and that assumption is not held when you have `gc.get_objects()`. Stop-the-world is not relevant here.
